sentences.text: Yes.
My uncle Jacob and his oldest son, Josef, otherwise known as Joshi-- that's how he was known.
He was nicknamed Joshi-- ended up in Lemberg, which at that time was occupied by Russia.
They were on the Russian side.
And of course, his wife and the youngest son were in Gorlice, which was occupied by the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They have-- the father-- I mean, Jacob and Joshi decided that they would like to be reunited with the rest of their family, so they have registered to go back.
They have registered with the local authorities-- with the local Russian authorities-- in Lemberg that they would like to reunite with their families on the German side.

Of course, the Russians took-- they registered them and told them, to the best of my knowledge, we will let you know when and if you will be free to go back to the German side and be reunited, but in the meantime, you have to stay here.
So they stayed there.
In the interim, we lived in Krakow under the German occupation.
And sometimes-- now we are, the Germans came into Krakow sometimes, I believe it was on September 3rd, 4th, or thereabouts.
We were living in Krakow until some time in late 1939 or early 1940.

A law, a German law came out that all these people who were not permanent residents of Krakow-- and I'm referring to Jewish people who were not permanent residents of Krakow, in other words the refugee, so to say, the ones that came to Krakow shortly before the war started-- had to leave Krakow.
And they gave us, more or less, I believe, a week or so to get out of Krakow.
So we had to go somewhere.
We couldn't go back to Cieszyn because Cieszyn was declared now part of the Third Reich.
It was Silesia.
It was territory which they claimed was really part of Germany, and they have annexed it.
Krawow was part of an area which was called at the time Grand Government of Poland [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] whereby we had my brother's wife, the one that stayed in Gorlice with her youngest son.
So we contacted her.
She says, sure.
You can come.
We'll find you an apartment.
And come to Gorlice.
You'll stay in Gorlice.
In Gorlice--
That was Jacob's wife, my aunt by marriage.
My father's-- my father's brother's wife.
I'll backtrack for a moment.
She herself was only with her youngest son because her husband and her oldest son were stuck in Lemberg, which was occupied by the Russians.
So we came over to go to Gorlice.
I mean, she found us an apartment, which we rented.
And we now lived in and Gorlice.
In the interim, they formed a ghetto in Gorlice, whereby the Jews had to live in a certain section.
Fortunately, the part that we lived in was part-- became later a part of the ghetto.
Therefore, we did not have to move.
So we actually lived in the Jewish part of Gorlice, and we, we, we were able to remain there.
Gorlice-- Cieszyn was a small town.
Gorlice was even smaller.
So you can well imagine what it was.
I think Gorlice must have had a total population of somewhere in the area of maybe 7,000, 8,000 at most.
But everybody knew everybody.
And it was very much a little typical, primitive type of Polish town.
The Jewish population that was there, of course, we stuck together.
And it was relatively easy to make friends for me because even though I was a newcomer, I was accepted very well by the other kids.
And we sort of-- we could not at that time attend school anymore.
As a matter of fact, the same holds true for Krakow.
Once the Germans marched into Krakow, the Jews were not permitted to attend school, public school or any other school.
So what we did-- actually, there were some Jewish teachers that were really professional teachers.
And they were not permitted to teach because they were Jews.
So they have decided to teach us-- banded together, and they have decided to give us children private lessons.
So what they did is as best as they could, they gave-- they continued school in private in somebody's home.
They knew how important it was for them to educate us.
I don't even think they were getting paid for it.
But it was-- being that they were dedicated teachers, they did it regardless.
And the same held true in Gorlice.
We were getting a bit of education from those teachers-- from those teachers which were really teachers, and they could no longer teach.
And they did it for the love of teaching.
And they were dedicated people, as I said before.
Pardon me?
Well, they were Jewish teachers.
And, of course, there were Jewish teachers, and they were teaching the Jewish children.
They did not have to teach the non-Jewish children because the non-Jewish children were permitted to go to school.
Their education was not interrupted.
It was only the Jewish children that couldn't go to Polish schools.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I-- as far as I can remember, they were even-- the Germans did permit to have classes for Jews in a Jewish school providing they were strictly for Jews.
There were no other people in there.
I don't know if was official or otherwise, but it was not done in a home.
It was done in a class.
No, I was in one of the school classes in Gorlice.
In Krakow, it was in a home class.
In Gorlice, it was in a Jewish type of-- it must have been part of a Jewish synagogue which was dedicated to be a Jewish school for the children.
So we were getting sort of an education in basic elementary education.
By the way, my education before the war, I finished six grades of elementary school, public school.
And of course, I was about to enter what we called gymnasium at the time, which is the equivalent of American high school.
But the war broke out, and I could not attend for the reason I stated before.
Well, in Gorlice, it was type of-- it was difficult, especially for refugees.
And we were refugees.
We really were not people that lived in Gorlice, so obviously, we did not have a business.
What we had to live on is-- we managed to bring some of our belongings.
And we had some jewelry that we were able to sell to support ourselves and so on and so forth.
So there was no income per se, except that we had to sell off some of our belongings which we were able to save when we left our hometown in Cieszyn.
But we managed, sort of.
So it wasn't-- it wasn't anything-- it wasn't a life of luxury, by any means.
But by the same token, we weren't starving, either.
We were able to secure food.
Now, food was rather difficult to obtain because it was the war years.
It was difficult to obtain, and I must say, it was difficult to obtain for the Gentile population as well as for the Jewish population.
It was much more difficult for the Jewish population because we were not permitted to go out to a farm and buy food, so we had to buy it locally, which made it more expensive and so on and so forth.
But, and of course, there was a shortage of food.
Everything was rationed.
So some of the food had to be secured on the black market.
And there always was a black market in those days where you could secure food, but at a price.
Well, the rules were-- by the time we got to Gorlice, there was really not a ghetto that was a closed-in ghetto.
It was sort of a ghetto whereby-- a designated area was for Jews.
And Jews had to stay in that area, although there was no wall.
There was no fence.
But we had to wear our arm bands.
And when a Jew was-- dared to cross over outside the designated area-- which was really, in effect-- it was really a ghetto, in effect-- he was severely punished.
He could have possibly even been shot on the spot.
But to the best of my knowledge, I don't think that any of the Jewish population ever wandered outside that area.
If they did, they took off their arm bands or they went-- they had to have outside contacts that helped them, one way or another.
But by and large, we lived within that designated area, and we had a what we call a Jewish community.
Now, that Jewish community was in charge for work details.
The Germans needed a certain group of people to do work outside the ghetto, wherever they needed us.
So it was the responsibility of the Jewish committee to supply these workers.
I was assigned-- I was at the time already 15 years old.
Now, we are now coming into 1942.
We are now somewhere in the late spring, early summer of 1942.
I was assigned to work in a German lumber yard.
I do remember the name.
The name of the lumber yard was Hobach Holzbau, which means it's a lumber yard that was not actually-- this was a lumber yard which was owned by a German company.
The name of the company was Hobach which is really an abbreviation of Holzbau, Ho-bach, first two letters.
Holz meaning wood, bau means building, so sort of a building.
Now, their primary function was to build barracks for the German army.
They were building parts of barracks, which were then shipped to wherever the soldiers needed them, and they were assembled on site.
Preassembled barracks.
And I worked in that part together with a group of other people.
I believe we were at that time possibly 50 or 60 young men that were working in that Hobach Holzbau, which was managed by a German with a big dog, a big shepherd, German Shepherd.
[LAUGHS] And he kept law and order with that dog.
If somebody was out of line, went out of line, that dog took care of you.
It was either Mr.-- his name was Mr.-- let me see if I can-- I may-- I may find his name a little later.
So let's forget his name for the moment.
But he kept a very tight rein.
He was a mean, mean character.
And the rest of the foremen, the people in that lumber yard were Poles who were working there.
They were employed.
They were salaried.
And we worked under the Polish administration, which was the inner administration.
I have seen one of my friends got bitten up by the dog.
He did something, some minor infraction.
He did get bitten up by the dog, not to a point where he was terribly disabled because the, the German boss, so to say, he just let the dog loose on him.
But he pulled the dog away after-- after he bit him a couple of times.
So I don't believe he was too badly hurt.
But this German was-- he had another-- not only that he let the dogs loose-- his dog.
It was only one dog.
But he, he had a something the equivalent of a baseball bat.
And he sort of did justice to us with his baseball bat for any infraction.
So he did-- I myself was never beaten by this particular person.
But some of my other people, of my coworkers, did get beaten up by him.
Pardon me?
Well, what happened is that one day-- and now I am already somewhere in August of 1942.
At the time, I was working in the night shift.
That place was so busy that they had a day shift, and they had a night shift.
I was assigned-- my group was the night shift.
Now, what we used to do is we were picked up by a truck at a certain point every day, approximately-- I believe it must've been around 4 o'clock in the afternoon.
And we worked for, I think it was 10 hours.
And every day, we were brought back in the morning, back home.
And then we could sleep and whatever.
At 4 o'clock, we had to be back at the designated place and go back for the next day's work.
Now, in August of 1942, while we were working in that lumber yard, we saw an entire fleet of trucks loaded.
It was the German SS going towards Gorlice.
Now, this lumber yard was approximately-- was in the suburbs of Gorlice.
We saw them at night, somewhere around 8:00, 9 o'clock.
They were all going en masse towards Gorlice.
So we expected-- we heard some rumors that they were going to liquidate the ghetto in Gorlice, and all the Jews were going to be taken away and sent out.
We didn't know where, but they were going to be sent out somewhere.
So when it came time to quit, we never came to the truck, but we hid in the lumber yard.
And there was lots of sawdust there, so we hid-- we each of us hid and somewhere else.
We scattered around it, and we hid, hoping that we would never be found.

And in the morning, we were hoping to escape, not to go back because the Poles told us that they're liquidating-- the Poles that were working with us there told us that they already got the news that there were-- that there was going to be-- that ghetto in Gorlice was going to be liquidated.
However, early in the morning, I myself fell asleep.
I was so tired from working there all night.
I fell asleep.
I was awakened by dogs sniffing me out.
They had dogs in there.
And wherever we were hidden, the dogs found us.
Now, the dogs did not attack us.
But they just brought us out in the open.
And we were loaded up in the morning on the trucks and taken to Gorlice.
When we came to Gorlice, we already saw that all the people in Gorlice were assembled in the marketplace.
They were-- and they were assembled.
They were all together.
They were the old people were in one area.
The middle-aged people were in another area.
Women and children were still in another area.
Actually, women and children and the old people were, were sort of together.
Us, the ones that worked in that Hobach, they separated us from the rest of the people.
There also was a factory near us where the lumber yard was.
There was a factory that was manufacturing tar paper.
Now, that tar paper, obviously, was used as part of those prefabricated barracks that we were making.
And there were-- there was a group of Jewish people working in that tar paper factory.
Now, they were also brought in.
We were brought in.
So what they did is they took us and they took the tar paper workers, and they united us.
And they separated us from the rest of the town.
Now, they have taken the rest of the Jews from wherever they were there.
They have taken them, and they loaded them up on trains.
They took them to the railroad station, loaded them on cattle trains.
Now, we could see them because that lumber yard was located right at that railroad station.
Obviously, lumber yards usually are located near railroad stations.
And we saw the Jewish population being loaded up.
Unfortunately, I never saw my parents because there were quite a number of them.
And I could never spot them at the time.
Maybe, perhaps when I got back-- when they took us back to work in that lumber yard, my parents might have been already loaded up into the closed sort of the cattle trains, cattle wagons.
And we could never-- I could never see them.
Pardon me?
Well, it's kind of difficult to sort of explain.
But we had the feeling that we were never going to see our parents again.
So that was-- it's a little difficult to explain what it was like.
But it was a feeling that-- we were hoping that they were really going to-- the story was that they were going to be taken over to another camp and for work.
But we had a feeling that this was not the case, even though we had no hard evidence at the time.
But the feeling was that these people were never going to survive the war.
They were going to be taken over and done away with.
The way they were treated.
The way we were treated.
And we knew that the basic feeling was-- the basic-- the final solution, we read about the final solution.
We knew what the final solution was.
I'd never read but people in our group did read And we were told in no uncertain terms that as soon as we are no longer needed, we will be destroyed.
Our only hope was that the war was going to end, and they never-- that it was going to end before they had a chance to destroy us all.
And for us, every day that we were under the occupation or in camp, it felt like a year.
Basically, a day can be a long time if you are under stress.
And it was under stress, every minute of it.
So we were in that camp, in that lumber yard.
And we worked in that lumber yard, continuing our things.
Our friends next door, which was the tar paper factory, they worked in the paper factory, in that tar paper factory.
And we stayed and worked in that place until, I would say this was-- now we are in-- they have liquidated the ghetto in Gorlice in August of 1942.
We continue working in that place until sometimes in I think it must've been either December of 1942 or possibly January of 1943.
By the way, my cousin who was Jacob's youngest son, who was with my aunt, he at the time was working.
He was very mechanically inclined.
So he was-- he got himself a job repairing automobiles for the German army.
So he was actually working for the German army in their garages as a-- he was a young man.
He was, I think, two years older than me, maybe a year or two years older.
But he was quite mechanically inclined.
So he was able to-- and he was already working for several months there.
So he and his mother were able to-- what happened is when the-- when the ghetto was liquidated, his mother was able to be put into the paper factory, the paper factory, that tar paper factory.
And he was still-- even though he was-- he came to-- even though during the day he worked for the German army repairing their trucks, but at night, he was-- he was sleeping in that paper factory.
And being that his mother was there also, so they were still together.
And he must've gotten permission for his mother to stay in that paper factory because he must have acquainted some of these Germans that he worked for.
And they used their pull to let him bring his mother to that paper factory, that tar paper factory.
So sometimes-- coming back now to late December or early January of 1943, they decided they no longer need us in that lumber yard.
So one day, they loaded us up on trucks.
And they have transported us to a town further inland in Poland called Rzeszow.
Rzeszow.
Had a relatively large Jewish population, and they had a ghetto.
So we were brought into the ghetto.
Not only were we brought into the ghetto, but I think within a week, they also decided that they no longer needed Jews in the tar paper factory.
So they took the people from the tar paper factory, and they also brought them to the ghetto in Rzeszow.
Of course, once you're in the ghetto, you are now subject to the Jewish community.
They had their Jewish sort of I wouldn't call them exactly collaborators, but they were the Jewish ghetto administration, which in some cases they may have been collaborators.
In some cases, they may not have been collaborators.
But in my experience, many, many of those people that worked in that committee were really straightforward people.
They tried to be fair, as fair as they could.
But some of them were collaborators, but many of them were not.
But in any event, we were assigned-- I was assigned to a room with many other people.
It is kind of difficult to remember how many because it was an apartment in the ghetto.
Now each apartment had a room.
And each room was-- we must have been to an apartment.
I would say there must've been maybe as many as 15 or 20 people in one apartment.
Some of them were with families, but many of them were not with families.
Many of them were just single men who managed to, so far, survive.
Their wives and children were taken away in one form or another.
